history.content: What is type 2 diabetes management?

Type 2 diabetes management focuses on lifestyle changes and medications for adults. Lifestyle changes include healthy eating with reduced refined carbohydrates, regular physical activity of about 150 minutes per week, and gradual weight loss if overweight. Common first-line medications for adults include metformin; other options include sulfonylureas, DPP-4 inhibitors and SGLT-2 inhibitors. Monitoring typically involves periodic HbA1c checks. This guidance is for adults with type 2 diabetes and is not for children or type 1 diabetes.

history.content: How do I manage high blood pressure at home?

For adults with mild to moderate hypertension, home management includes reducing dietary salt to under 5 grams a day, regular aerobic activity, limiting alcohol, quitting smoking, and monitoring blood pressure at home. A GP usually confirms the diagnosis with repeated readings before prescribing medications. This guidance is framed around non-pregnant adults; pregnant women, children, and patients with kidney disease need separate specialist advice.

notes: Prior answer explicitly says non-pregnant adults. Clarification should confirm that scope.

history.content: When should I go to a health post for a fever?

For an otherwise healthy adult, a fever under 38.5 degrees Celsius for under three days with no red flags can be managed at home with rest, fluids and paracetamol. A visit to the nearest health post is advised if the fever lasts more than three days, goes above 39 degrees, or is accompanied by warning signs like severe headache, neck stiffness, difficulty breathing, persistent vomiting, or confusion. Infants under 3 months, pregnant women, and people with chronic illness should seek care earlier regardless of temperature.

history.content: How do I treat a common cold at home?

For a mild common cold in adults, home measures include rest, drinking plenty of warm fluids, saline nasal rinses, honey and warm water for a sore throat (not for children under 1 year), and paracetamol for fever or aches if needed. Most colds resolve in about 7–10 days. See a doctor if symptoms worsen after a week, if breathing becomes difficult, or if there is high fever, chest pain, or blood in the sputum.

history.content: How is ORS prepared and used for diarrhoea?

Oral rehydration solution (ORS) is prepared by dissolving one standard WHO ORS sachet in 1 litre of clean drinking water. Give small frequent sips to a person with diarrhoea — adults can take up to 200 ml after each loose stool, and children need roughly 50–100 ml after each loose stool, under caregiver supervision. Continue normal feeding; infants should continue breastfeeding. Seek medical care if the person cannot keep fluids down, has blood in stool, is very drowsy, or has sunken eyes or no urine for many hours.

history.content: What are the early warning signs of TB I should watch for?

Common early signs of pulmonary tuberculosis in adults include a cough lasting more than two weeks, coughing up blood or blood-stained sputum, unexplained weight loss, night sweats, loss of appetite, and a low-grade fever. TB can also affect lymph nodes or other organs with different symptoms. Anyone with a cough lasting over two weeks in Nepal is recommended to get a sputum test at a DOTS centre or health post — TB diagnosis and treatment are free under the national programme.

history.content: What lifestyle changes help lower blood sugar?

For adults with type 2 diabetes or prediabetes, helpful lifestyle changes include: eating more vegetables and whole grains while reducing refined sugar and white rice; walking or other moderate activity most days of the week; losing 5–10 percent of body weight if overweight; limiting alcohol; quitting tobacco; and getting good sleep. These changes work alongside — not instead of — medications prescribed by a doctor. Type 1 diabetes is managed primarily with insulin and requires specialist care, though lifestyle still supports overall health.

history.content: How much physical activity is recommended for a healthy adult?

For a generally healthy adult aged 18–64, WHO recommends at least 150–300 minutes per week of moderate-intensity aerobic activity, or 75–150 minutes of vigorous activity, plus muscle-strengthening on two or more days per week. People who are currently inactive should start gradually and build up. Adults over 65 are advised to add balance activities. Separate guidance exists for children, pregnant women, and people with chronic conditions, and anyone with a heart or joint condition should check with a doctor before starting.

history.content: What should I do for a sore throat at home?

For a mild sore throat in adults, home measures include drinking warm fluids, gargling with warm salt water, sucking on lozenges, resting your voice, and taking paracetamol if needed for pain. Most sore throats are viral and get better within a week. See a doctor if the sore throat lasts more than a week, is very severe, makes swallowing or breathing difficult, comes with a rash or high fever, or if you notice white patches on the tonsils or swollen neck glands.

history.content: How do I know if my blood pressure reading is high?

For most non-pregnant adults, a blood pressure reading consistently at or above 140/90 mmHg measured on more than one occasion is generally considered high, according to common primary-care guidance. A single high reading at home is not enough to diagnose hypertension — it needs confirmation by a clinician with repeated measurements, ideally including home or ambulatory monitoring. Readings taken after exercise, caffeine, or stress can be transiently elevated. Thresholds differ for pregnant women, children, and people with kidney disease or diabetes, who should use clinician-set targets.

history.content: What is type 2 diabetes and how is it usually managed?

Type 2 diabetes is a chronic condition in which the body does not use insulin effectively, leading to high blood sugar. It is commonly associated with overweight, physical inactivity and family history. Management generally combines lifestyle changes — healthy eating, regular activity, and weight loss — with medications such as metformin as a common first-line option. Regular follow-up with a clinician and periodic HbA1c checks are part of long-term care.

history.content: What are the common symptoms of fever in adults?

Common symptoms accompanying fever in adults include feeling hot, chills or shivering, headache, muscle aches, tiredness and loss of appetite. A temperature at or above 38 degrees Celsius is generally considered a fever. Most short-lived fevers are caused by viral infections and settle within a few days with rest and fluids. Warning signs that warrant seeing a doctor promptly include a fever lasting more than three days, very high temperatures, severe headache with neck stiffness, breathing difficulty, persistent vomiting, confusion, or a rash that does not fade under pressure.

history.content: What causes a runny nose?

A runny nose is most commonly caused by viral upper respiratory infections like the common cold, allergic rhinitis triggered by dust, pollen or animal dander, sinus irritation from dry or cold air, and sometimes structural issues like a deviated septum. Persistent runny nose with thick coloured discharge or facial pain can point to sinusitis. Most short-lived runny noses in otherwise well people resolve on their own within about a week.

history.content: What are the warning signs of dehydration?

Warning signs of dehydration include dry mouth and tongue, thirst, reduced urine output or dark-coloured urine, tiredness, dizziness on standing, and sunken eyes. In infants and young children, additional signs include a sunken soft spot on the head, crying without tears, and unusual drowsiness or irritability. Severe dehydration with confusion, very fast breathing, or no urine for many hours is a medical emergency that needs hospital care.
